summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her recovery experience one week after bariatric sleeve surgery, having just stopped pain medication. She critiques online resources that minimize the surgery's severity by focusing on incision pain rather than the intense internal pain from having most of her stomach removed. Despite moments of regret and panic during recovery, she reports rapid improvement and no lasting regret. The post concludes with her decision to reinvent her wardrobe entirely in goth style as she loses weight.
key_points: Online medical resources fundamentally misrepresent bariatric surgery recovery by focusing on incision pain when 99.5% of pain comes from internal trauma
The surgery is marketed as minimally invasive (laparoscopic, one-day hospital stay) but is actually 'a crude and unsparing act'
Recovery improves dramatically day by day - each night was 'tons better' than the previous one
She frames the weight loss as a health decision (avoiding diabetes, enabling exercise) rather than aesthetic goals
She's using the body transformation as an opportunity to completely reinvent her personal style
money_quotes: I had most of my stomach yanked out and the remainder tied together with twine.
All of my nerves inside my body are well aware of this fact and stayed busy for at least five days continuously notifying me of this act of brutality.
So, long story short, the first real observation about my bariatric sleeve surgery is this: it's a crude and unsparing act.
They're the same assholes who talk about how they orgasmed during childbirth.
I think the actual situation is that those websites are written by people who haven't actually experienced the surgery themselves.
It's amazing, actually, how quickly we can heal.
I don't care. The point of this was to avoid diabetes and be able to stay healthy and exercise, even in the summer.
key_insight: Medical information sources often sanitize or minimize the reality of procedures because they're written by people who haven't actually experienced them.
